prompt: Explain in two short sentences why CPU interference can increase inference latency on an embedded board.
reference_for_bertscore: CPU interference can increase inference latency because the model shares cores, caches and memory bandwidth with other processes. This contention delays prompt processing and token generation.
output: CPU interference can increase inference latency on an embedded board because the instructions and data from the CPU may interfere with the instructions and data from the FPGA in the memory. This can cause unexpected errors and delays in the inference process, leading to higher latency.

prompt: Explain the difference between throughput and latency in AI inference using one short paragraph.
reference_for_bertscore: Latency measures how long one inference takes from input to output, while throughput measures how many tokens or requests are processed per unit of time. A system can have high throughput but still be unsuitable for real-time use if individual responses arrive too late.
output: In AI inference, throughput refers to the total number of samples that an AI system processes within a specified time frame, while latency indicates the time it takes for an AI model to receive and execute a prediction. In short, throughput measures the ability of an AI system to process a large number of samples, while latency refers to the time it takes for predictions to be generated. Both measures are crucial for the performance and efficiency of AI models in real-world applications.

output: Throughput refers to the rate at which data is processed in AI inference, while latency is the time required to complete the data processing. Throughput measures the number of operations or data points that can be processed in a unit of time, typically in a batch or stream. Latency indicates how long it takes to send or receive a piece of data, often measured in milliseconds or seconds. In AI inference, high throughput and low latency are key factors for improving system performance, making efficient data processing and communication an essential consideration in real-world applications.
